summary: A 1994 abstract co-authored by Julie E. Buring highlighting the severe underutilization of low-dose aspirin in treating acute myocardial infarction, despite its proven efficacy, safety, and low cost.

principles.statement: Low-dose aspirin should be widely administered during acute evolving myocardial infarction.
principles.rationale: It decreases mortality by about a quarter, has a highly favorable safety profile, can be given to almost all patients, and provides comparable benefits to thrombolytic agents at a fraction of the cost.
principles.source_quote: Aspirin can be given to virtually all patients, has a far more favorable safety profile than thrombolysis, and confers a comparable benefit at a small fraction of the cost of thrombolytic agents.

signature_quotes.text: The more widespread use of aspirin in acute MI is one of the most important and timely clinical challenges in the United States.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding the abstract on the underutilization of aspirin for acute myocardial infarction.

anti_patterns.description: Underutilizing low-dose aspirin and thrombolytic therapy during acute evolving myocardial infarction.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It represents a failure to deploy a highly accessible, safe, and cheap intervention that is proven to decrease mortality by about one quarter.
